upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a remarkably warm 850mb temperature of +15.6°C at 1520m, creating a strong inversion that effectively caps thermals well below ridge height at most inland sites — the surface-to-850mb lapse rate of only -1.19°C/km is far below dry adiabatic and indicates a very stable airmass with suppressed convection. The surface spread at KOAK is only 2.6°C, suggesting residual low-level moisture, while 850mb spread jumps to 19°C confirming the inversion is real and sharp. Upper winds at 700mb are light (8 kts from 285°) and 500mb shows 14 kts from SW — no significant wind shear hazard aloft, but the stable profile means zero thermal development today.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KHAF reporting NW 13-15 kts and CLR by mid-afternoon — sea breeze well established at coast. KRHV showing 330° at 7-10 kts with SKC/SCT080 and 30°C — classic warm inland afternoon confirming valley heating, though the stable sounding means thermals are not developing despite surface warmth. KWVI 260° at 8 kts, KAPC 220° at 4 kts — light inland flow. KSFO calm to 4 kts with BKN200 high cloud deck. No precipitation, no turbulence advisories.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NW/N-facing launch, thermal/valley breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 launches into the Santa Clara Valley thermal cycle; NW valley breeze fills in by mid-morning, combining with orographic lift off the Diablo Range. Ideal conditions are a W-NW 8-14 kt surface wind at launch with a thermal ceiling well above 1750 ft.
sites.todaySetup: Today's deciding problem at the high launch is that the boundary layer is capped by a powerful low-level inversion — the sounding puts 850mb at +15.6°C, which effectively kills thermal development despite reasonable surface heating. Winds at launch are NNE 9-12 kts through most of the morning and midday (HRRR and GFS agree: 10-12 kts from 19-38° at 10m, 12-14 kts at 80m), which is cross to slightly wrong direction for the primary NW-facing launch. The wind direction gradually backs toward N and then NW by late afternoon (352° by 1600, 325° by 1700), but by then clouds build to 86-100% cover and the sky goes completely overcast. No CAPE, zero precipitation, and a BL height that only reaches 815m (about 2600 ft MSL) by 1400 — barely above the 1750 ft launch elevation, meaning there is effectively no thermal ceiling to work with. The afternoon cloud deck further suppresses any remaining surface heating. This is a no-fly day at the high launch: wrong wind direction early, sky socks in late, and thermals are nonexistent all day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 9-12 kts at the ridge — wrong direction for NW launch, BL height only 110-410m. Stable and crossed; do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds back slightly toward N, 10-11 kts, BL creeps to 490-815m but cloud cover starts building to 20-56%. Still no thermal development and direction remains unfavorable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind direction finally reaches NW-N (352-325°) by 1600-1700 but cloud cover hits 86-100% by 1500 and stays socked in. Flyable direction arrives too late with no sky and no lift.

verdict.wind: 10 kts NNE (wrong direction until sky closes)
verdict.hazards: Cross/wrong-direction launch winds all morning, full overcast by 1500, inversion caps BL near launch elevation, no thermals

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, sheltered valley bowl, beginner/thermal site
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch at Ed Levin is a south-facing training hill that works best on light N-NW valley breeze and weak thermals in the early afternoon. It requires gentle conditions — ideal for P2 students when winds are 5-10 kts from a northerly or westerly direction.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch faces a similar story to the high site but with even lighter winds. HRRR shows NNE surface flow at 7-10 kts early (8-9 AM), dropping to 6-9 kts and slowly backing toward N by midday. The wind direction from 7-19° is acceptable for the launch, and the lower launch elevation means the BL height (375-1370m by afternoon) is more meaningfully above the site — but CAPE is zero and the stable sounding means thermals are weak-to-absent. The real deal-breaker is the progressive cloud buildup: skies go from clear at 10 AM to 25% at 2 PM to 87% by 3 PM and 100% by 4 PM. Additionally, winds at 80m (10-11 kts) show more speed than surface, suggesting a stable shear layer just above the training hill. There is a narrow workable window midday when direction is acceptable and skies are still partly clear, but thermal lift will be minimal. Suitable only for sledder/scratch flights; any soaring will be brief and weak.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 7-10 kts at surface — direction OK for 600 ft launch. BL shallow (125-505m) and no thermals. Sledder conditions at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease to 6-9 kts NNE, BL expands to 650-1210m. Skies still 0-25% cloud. Best window for weak thermal or scratchy ridge work — marginal soaring possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud cover explodes to 87-100% by 1500, completely shutting off any remaining solar heating. Wind backs to N then NW. Flights become pure sledders with no lift source.

verdict.hazards: Overcast by mid-afternoon kills solar heating, stable inversion limits ceiling, 80m winds slightly stronger than surface (shear), no thermal development

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal sea cliff, W-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site powered by the Pacific sea breeze pushing up the west-facing coastal bluff. It needs 10-20 kts from W-NW at the cliff to generate reliable ridge lift; too light and you're a sledder into the LZ, too strong and rotor/turbulence becomes the hazard.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is under-powered today — the key problem is that the sea breeze is too light and variable to generate usable ridge lift. HRRR shows surface winds starting from SE/E (4.5 kts from 124°) at 8 AM, going nearly calm (0.2 kts) at 10 AM, then chaotically variable through midday (2 kts from NE at 11 AM, 2 kts from N at noon) before finally shifting to NW-W by 1-2 PM at only 4-5 kts. The 80m winds tell a similar story — only 1.9-5 kts through noon, reaching 6-7 kts by late afternoon. This is well below the 10+ kt threshold needed for reliable ridge soaring. There is a notable CAPE spike of 170 at noon in the GFS model — an unusual flag at a coastal site suggesting a brief convective instability as the air masses transition, which could generate brief gusty/unpredictable conditions. The buoy data confirms the context: SST is a cold 13.3°C at Half Moon Bay with 9-second swell at 1.0m, and offshore winds are 9.7 kts from 300-310° at the buoys — the marine layer is present but the gradient is not strong enough to drive a robust sea breeze onshore. KHAF METARs show NW 10-15 kts at the coast, but that flow is not translating effectively to the cliff face. By late afternoon (1500-1600), 97-100% cloud cover moves in. Not enough wind at the right direction and time for safe ridge soaring today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds from SE/E then nearly calm — completely wrong direction for ridge lift, rotor risk off the cliff. Do not attempt.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind transitions erratically through N, NW, W — 2-5 kts. Chaotic direction changes, light and variable, plus a GFS CAPE spike at noon suggesting instability. Unpredictable and too light.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW-W flow finally arrives at 4-5 kts by 1300-1400 but cloud cover hits 75-100% by 1400-1500. Winds remain well below ridge soaring threshold throughout.

verdict.hazards: Winds too light for ridge lift, erratic direction shifts through midday, brief CAPE spike at noon, full overcast by 1500, rotor risk on wrong-direction flow

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland peak, NW-facing launch, thermal and ridge hybrid site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on the same Diablo Range thermal/valley breeze cycle as Ed Levin but is more exposed. The NW-facing launch needs W-NW flow from the Bay and sufficient thermal development to top-land or work the ridge. Ideal conditions are 8-15 kts NW with a BL top well above the 2500 ft peak.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same fatal flaw as Ed Levin today: the wind direction is NNE 9-12 kts all morning through midday, which is cross to the NW-facing launch and would put pilots in rotor behind the ridge. The GFS and HRRR models are in full agreement — 10-12 kts from 19-38° at 10m, 12-15 kts at 80m from morning through 2 PM. This is not a launchable direction. The boundary layer, despite reaching 815m by 1400 and 1170m by 1500, is capped by the powerful 850mb inversion (+15.6°C) meaning thermals exist only below this shallow layer. By the time wind direction finally backs to N and NW (4° by 1500, 352° by 1600), cloud cover has already reached 86% at 1500 and 100% by 1600, shutting off all solar input. The combination of wrong launch direction all day, rapidly building overcast, and a stable capping inversion makes Mission Peak unflyable today. The NNE flow could also create mechanical turbulence on the lee side of the peak for anyone attempting to launch into it.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 9-12 kts — directly cross to NW launch, rotor risk on the lee. BL only 110-410m. Do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind holds NNE 10-11 kts, BL reaches 400-815m but still capped well below useful ceiling. Overcast begins building. Launch direction remains wrong.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Direction finally backs to N-NW by 1500-1600 but sky goes 86-100% overcast simultaneously. No thermal energy left and lift source disappears.

verdict.hazards: Cross/wrong-direction winds at NW launch all day, rotor risk behind peak, overcast by 1500, stable inversion caps thermals near launch elevation

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks like a total washout at the high site. GFS shows 100% cloud cover all day with light W-NW winds of 0-8 kts and BL heights staying below 520m. Tomorrow.io agrees with socked-in overcast. No thermal development possible and there may be light precipitation by afternoon (1-2% precip probability). NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same overcast story — 100% cloud cover all day per both GFS and HRRR, winds calm to 7 kts W-NW. No solar heating, no thermals, BL below 550m. The only slight positive is winds shift to NW by evening at 10-12 kts, but visibility and overcast make it a non-starter. NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Monday shows persistent overcast (94-100%) and light W-SW winds of 4-7 kts at surface, reaching 7-8 kts at 80m. This is marginal for ridge lift — slightly better than Sunday but still below the reliable threshold. Cloud cover makes it unpleasant and visibility may be reduced. Late afternoon/evening sees winds pick up to 10 kts from W, which could be worth monitoring, but 100% cloud cover is the limiting factor. MARGINAL at best.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Monday is socked in with 100% overcast all day. Winds are light and variable (calm to 7 kts) shifting W-NW. Precipitation probability rises to 1-2% by afternoon. No viable thermal or ridge soaring. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday (March 31) shows continued 100% overcast with light S-SW winds and CAPE values of 17-30 with precip probability of 15-33%. This is an active weather day — potential for unstable showers. Not a flying day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday mirrors the high site — overcast, southerly flow, CAPE up to 30, and 15-33% precipitation probability. Possible convective activity in the afternoon. NO FLY and potentially dangerous.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday shows 98-100% cloud cover all day with southerly/SW winds of 5-13 kts. Light rain possible (22-31% precip probability). The 13.4 kts from 246° at 8 AM is approaching usable for ridge but in rainy/cloudy conditions it's irrelevant. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday has similar conditions — overcast, S-SW flow, CAPE developing, and precip probability climbing through the day. Shower risk by afternoon. NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday (April 1) shows a more interesting pattern — southerly flow 7-11 kts with BL heights reaching 1485-1700m and CAPE up to 30. However, 100% cloud cover at 10 AM and 14:00, with 25-33% precip probability and strong 80m winds of 12-18 kts from SW by afternoon suggest an active frontal/post-frontal pattern. Could be a dynamic day but with thunderstorm risk — P4 locals only if it clears.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday has the same active pattern — S-SW winds building to 10-11 kts, BL to 1700m, CAPE 10-30, and 25% precip probability with very strong 80m winds (17-18 kts SW by 1600). Not suitable for normal operations — convective risk.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Wednesday shows southerly 10-14 kts at surface with CAPE 30 and 35-44% precip probability. Winds eventually back to SW-W at 11-12 kts. If the system clears earlier than forecast and winds shift NW, there could be a brief post-frontal window, but precip probability and CAPE make this speculative. Monitor closely.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Wednesday shows similar active weather — S-SW 6-11 kts, BL to 1530m, CAPE up to 40, 26-32% precip probability with 80m winds reaching 15-16 kts SW. Convective risk is real. Not a flying day under current forecast — conditions would need significant revision to consider flying.

watchlist: CAPE spike of 170 J/kg flagged at Mussel Rock at noon today (Sunday) — watch for brief convective instability and erratic gusty conditions at the coast around midday
Strong 850mb inversion (+15.6°C) is the dominant feature suppressing all thermal activity today — this cap is unusually warm for late March and worth watching for persistence into the week
Marine layer SST at 13.1-13.3°C (very cold) — may deepen the marine influence and delay any sea breeze onset if the inversion weakens mid-week
Precipitation probability rises significantly Tuesday into Wednesday with CAPE developing — potential for convective showers or embedded thunderstorms; check AIRMET Sierra for Tuesday
Wednesday April 1 post-frontal pattern could offer a brief fly window if clearing occurs early — monitor KHAF METAR and buoy winds for NW sea breeze recovery
tldr: Today is essentially a no-fly day across all Bay Area sites — a powerful low-level inversion kills thermals, NNE winds are wrong direction for every inland launch, and cloud cover socks in by mid-afternoon. Your only remote option is Ed Levin 600 ft for a quick sledder between 11 AM and 1 PM if you just need to fly something. The week ahead looks messy with overcast Monday, possible showers Tuesday, and an active frontal system Wednesday — keep an eye on Wednesday afternoon for a potential post-frontal window.
